title: Christ's Return
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Two Kinds of Professing Christians
subpoints: Paul distinguishes between earthly Christians (verse 18-19) whose minds are set on earthly things — enemies of the cross whose end is destruction — and heavenly Christians (verse 20) who live with eyes on the life to come

This is the third sermon in a short Advent series: the first covered Old Testament prophecy pointing to Christ, the second covered the historical Christ from the Gospel with theological implications, this afternoon covers his second Advent — his return
Focus: the desire for and mechanics of the resurrection of the body in light of the Savior's return

outline.title: 1. Our Citizenship Is in Heaven
subpoints: Verse 20: our citizenship, our allegiance, is in heaven — we live according to the dictates of the King of that country; this life is a sojourn and a pilgrimage toward that country
From that country, the Savior and Lord Jesus Christ is coming to meet us — those who are citizens of heaven eagerly wait for the King of that country to come and transform them
He will transform our lowly bodies (literally "bodies of humiliation") to be conformed to his glorious body — by the same power with which he is able to subdue all things to himself

outline.title: 2. The Lowly Body: What We Experience Now
subpoints: Our bodies are lowly — we experience the effects of sin; we age, get sick, hurt, lose mental capacity, and die; all examples of the body of humiliation brought about by the fall
When Christ comes to consummate the redemption of all creation, he will remove the effects of the fall from our bodies

1 Corinthians 15:42-53 — the body is sown in corruption, raised in incorruption; sown in dishonor, raised in glory; sown in weakness, raised in power; sown a natural body, raised a spiritual body; we shall all be changed in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye

outline.title: 3. The Mechanics of the Resurrection
subpoints: The resurrected body will no longer be able to sin — a state better than the original creation; Adam and Eve were able to sin and able not to sin; at the resurrection we will not be able to sin
Christ has not yet returned — the transformation has not yet taken place; it will occur after a time of great struggle described in Revelation

1 Thessalonians 4:13-17 — those who died from the beginning of the world to Christ's coming will be raised first; then those who are alive will be transformed without experiencing death; in the twinkling of an eye

How is this possible? By the power that enables Christ to subdue all things to himself — if he can subdue all things, raising people who have been dead for thousands of years is relatively simple by comparison

Why focus on the resurrection rather than the intermediate state in heaven? Because heaven is the place where we go as souls between death and resurrection; real heaven is the physical place with Christ after the resurrection; right now we relate to Christ through our souls, but in the resurrection we will relate to him as whole persons — soul and body together; that is what we crave

outline.title: 4. How the Coming of the King Affects Us Now
subpoints: Should cause us to eagerly wait — Romans 8:19: the entire creation eagerly waits for the revealing of the sons of God; we should have the same earnest expectation, saturating our thoughts

Should cause us to persevere to the end — Romans 8:25: if we hope for what we do not see, we eagerly wait for it with perseverance; keeping our eyes on Christ's coming helps us get to the end of this life
Should cause us to live an obedient life — 1 Corinthians 15:58: therefore be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that your labor is not in vain

Should be a source of great encouragement — 1 Thessalonians 4: Paul says these things about the last days so that we may encourage one another; two constant themes in 1 Thessalonians on the return of Christ: encouragement and obedience in the present life
Should cause us to stand firm — Philippians 4:1: therefore, my beloved brethren, stand fast in the Lord; at Christ's return we will reach the mark for which we pressed forward our whole life

For those not in Christ: his return is not a comfort but a dread; he comes not to bless but to judge; but today is a day of salvation — trust in Christ and join those looking forward to that glorious day

summary: This afternoon sermon completes a three-part Advent series at Olympia Bible Presbyterian Church. The first sermon covered Old Testament prophecy pointing to Christ, the morning sermon covered the historical Christ and the theological necessity of the Incarnation from John 3, and this sermon focuses on Christ's second Advent — his return — drawing on Philippians 3:17-4:1. Pastor Lyro opens by distinguishing Paul's two types of professing Christians: earthly Christians whose minds and lives are entirely oriented to this world, enemies of the cross whose end is destruction; and heavenly Christians who live now with their eyes on the life to come. The sermon then develops the content of what the heavenly Christian is waiting for: not merely heaven as an intermediate state of souls, but the full resurrection — the transformation of the body of humiliation into a glorious body like Christ's at his return. Using 1 Corinthians 15 and 1 Thessalonians 4, the mechanics of the resurrection are explained: the dead in Christ raised first, the living transformed in the twinkling of an eye, all by the same power that enables Christ to subdue all things. The sermon argues that Christians should desire the resurrection rather than merely the intermediate state because we were made as whole persons — soul and body — and the resurrection is the final declaration of our adoption as sons and daughters of God. Four practical effects of keeping the coming of the King in view are drawn out: eagerly waiting, persevering to the end, living obediently, and being encouraged — culminating in the command of 4:1 to stand fast in the Lord.
transcript:
## Introduction: Two Kinds of Professing Christians

Philippians chapter 3, reading verses 17 through chapter 4 verse 1: *Brethren, join in following my example, and note those who so walk as you have us for a pattern. For many walk, of whom I have told you often, and now tell you even weeping, that they are the enemies of the cross of Christ: whose end is destruction, whose god is their belly, and whose glory is in their shame — who set their mind on earthly things. For our citizenship is in heaven, from which we also eagerly wait for the Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ, who will transform our lowly body that it may be conformed to his glorious body, according to the working by which he is able even to subdue all things to himself. Therefore, my beloved and longed-for brethren, my joy and crown, so stand fast in the Lord, beloved.*

According to Paul there are two kinds of people who profess to believe in Christ Jesus for their salvation.

One type he calls earthly Christians — whose minds are only on the things of this world, though they claim to be following Jesus. In reality they are not believers at all but enemies of the cross, whose end is eternity in hell if they do not repent and truly trust in Christ.

The other type Paul labels heavenly Christians — always want to be on the heavenly side; just a hint before you fall asleep, heavenly is good. These are the ones who live their lives now with their eyes on the life to come. A major trait of the heavenly Christian is his desire for the return of Jesus Christ to transform his body and soul to be like Christ.

This afternoon is our third sermon in a short Advent series. We started with Old Testament prophecy pointing to Christ. This morning we talked about the historical Christ from the Gospel with theological implication. This afternoon we talk about his second Advent — his return — and our desire for the resurrection of the body in light of that.

## 1. Our Citizenship Is in Heaven

Paul states in verse 20 that our citizenship, our allegiance, is in heaven — and we live according to the dictates of the King of that country. We are citizens of another country; this life is a sojourn, a pilgrimage toward that other country.

From this country, from heaven, the Savior and Lord Jesus Christ is coming to meet us. Those who are citizens of heaven eagerly wait for the day when the King of that country is coming to meet them. They are looking forward to it because he will transform them — from lowly to glorious, from humble to glorious — by the same power with which he is able to subdue all things to himself.

## 2. The Lowly Body

Paul calls our bodies in verse 21 lowly bodies — literally bodies of humiliation. We know that, right? We experience the effects of sin: we age, get sick, hurt, lose our mental capacities, and die. All of this is the result of the fall played out in our bodies. When I played basketball the other day, I know I look like a specimen of physical fitness but I am hurting today.

When Christ comes back to consummate the redemption of all creation, to finalize the last step in consummating the redemption of all creation, he will remove the effects of the fall from our bodies.

1 Corinthians 15:42-53: *So also is the resurrection of the dead. The body is sown in corruption, it is raised in incorruption; it is sown in dishonor, it is raised in glory; it is sown in weakness, it is raised in power; it is sown a natural body, it is raised a spiritual body... Behold, I tell you a mystery: we shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed — in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound, and the dead will be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed. For this corruptible must put on incorruption, and this mortal must put on immortality.*

Christ the Creator will recreate our bodies to be like his own glorious body at his coming.

The resurrected body will no longer be able to sin — and that is a better state than the original creation. Adam and Eve were able to sin and able not to sin. At the resurrection we will not be able to sin. That is going to be an unchangeable state, and how glorious that will be.

## 3. The Mechanics of the Resurrection

Christ has not yet returned. The transformation of the body has not yet taken place. The return of Christ will take place at a future time following a period of great struggle the church will have to go through, as described in Revelation.

How is it going to take place? Verse 21 — by the power that enables him even to subject all things to himself. Paul is using what is called an a fortiori argument from the greater to the lesser. Jesus is going to subdue all things — every created being is going to belong to Jesus Christ. If he has the power to do that, raising a few billion people is relatively simple by comparison. He can do that in his sleep, as it were.

1 Thessalonians 4:13-17: Paul says do not be ignorant concerning those who have fallen asleep, lest you sorrow as others who have no hope. For we believe that Jesus died and rose again, and God will bring with him those who sleep in Jesus. We who are alive and remain until the coming of the Lord will by no means precede those who are asleep. For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a shout, and the dead in Christ will rise first. Then we who are alive and remain will be caught up together with them in the clouds.

Those who are dead at the coming of Christ will be raised first, then those who are alive will be transformed without experiencing death — in the twinkling of an eye. Not a Sunday afternoon blink — those are much longer. In the twinkling of a normal eye.

Some ask: why focus on the resurrection rather than heaven itself as the object of Christian expectation? The answer is that heaven — when we think of it — is the intermediate state, the place where we go as souls between death and the coming of Christ. Real heaven is the physical place with Christ after the resurrection. Right now we relate to Christ through our souls. In the resurrection we relate to Christ as whole persons — soul and body together. We were made to be body and soul together, and when either part is missing we are incomplete. The resurrection will be the final marriage between a perfect body and a perfect soul, forever. And at the return of the King and the resurrection of the body, we will fully enter into the glorious riches of being a child of God.

## 4. How the Coming of the King Affects Us Now

First: it should cause us to eagerly wait for Christ's return and the resurrection. Romans 8:19 — the entire creation eagerly waits for the revealing of the sons of God. Creation — the stars, the planets, every molecule, every animal, every tree — is waiting for our resurrection, because when we are raised up the entire universe will be freed from the curse of the fall. We should have that same earnest expectation, saturating our thoughts.

Second: it should cause us to persevere to the end. Romans 8:25 — if we hope for what we do not see, we eagerly wait for it with perseverance. Keeping our eyes upon the coming of Christ will help us get to the end of this life. This is how God preserves his saints.

Third: it should cause us to live an obedient life. At the end of 1 Corinthians 15, after a long time talking about the resurrection, Paul ends: *therefore be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that your labor is not in vain in the Lord.*

Fourth: it should be a source of great encouragement. 1 Thessalonians 4 — Paul tells us these things about the last days so that we may encourage one another with these words. The two constant themes of 1 Thessalonians on the doctrine of Christ's return: encouragement and obedience in the present life.

And lastly: it should cause us to stand firm. That is how Paul ends in chapter 4 verse 1: *therefore, my beloved and longed-for brethren, my joy and crown — stand fast in the Lord.* At the return of Christ, we will reach the mark for which we pressed forward during our whole life. That encourages us to keep going.

So if you are in Christ Jesus, if you are trusting him for the forgiveness of your sins — let the return of the King and the resurrection of the body be an encouragement to you. Think about that glorious day. In days of despair, think about that day. In days when things are not going well, think about that day. In glorious times of worship, think about how heaven will be infinitely better even than that.

And if you are not in Christ Jesus, the only thing you can do is dread his return. He will not be coming to bless you. He will be coming to judge you. But today is not that day yet. Today is a day of salvation. Hear the gospel, trust in Christ, submit your life to the rule of King Jesus — and then join the rest of us looking forward to his coming.
